themes.name: Myths, Marble & Half-Truths
themes.tagline: Everything you learned in history class is about to get a serious fact-check.

items.title: How Odysseus Took His Revenge
items.description:
When Odysseus finally returns to Ithaca, his homecoming turns into a ruthless reckoning. Tristan Hughes and Dr Emily Hauser dive into the shocking climax of Homer’s Odyssey, where hospitality, vengeance and survival collide in a blood-soaked finale. 

What drives Odysseus to unleash such fury, and what does the massacre reveal about power, justice and ancient heroism?

We're going on *TOUR* to Australia and New Zealand! - final tickets are available here:
https://fane.com.au/whats-on/an-evening-

items.title: Seven Wonders of the Ancient World - The Truth Behind the Legends
items.description:
Six of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World are gone. But almost everything you think you know about them is wrong.

The Hanging Gardens of Babylon may never have existed. The famous image of the Colossus of Rhodes straddling the harbour? Invented by medieval artists 1,400 years after it fell. The pyramids weren't built by slaves. And several of these "lost" wonders aren't really lost — pieces of them are hiding inside buildings you can visit today.

In this video, we go beyond the legends and

items.title: How Did Medieval Women Style Their Hair?
items.description:
In the medieval period, society attached a strong importance to appearing healthy and clean. Skin, hair and cleanliness was a measure of your health, and your status, and for women, their fertility too. In this video, Historic Kitchens Experts Natalie and Anya delve into the world of 14th century hair and beauty, and try out a few recipes from the time. Despite ingredients such as egg yolk, lard and white wine, Anya and Natalie are both impressed with the results.

Find out what the Historic Kit

items.title: Amsterdam - 1667
items.description:
Step into 17th-century Amsterdam with an AI reconstruction inspired by period maps, paintings, and city records from the Dutch Golden Age. These scenes draw on VOC archives, canal plans, and contemporary sketches, then reinterpret them through AI while staying true to the materials and layouts of the time.

During this century, Amsterdam grew into a major trading capital: its harbor pulsed with global commerce, the canal rings expanded, and places like Dam Square, the Herengracht, and the Oost-I

items.title: 7 Jewellery Myths You Still Believe
items.description:
Jewellery is sold through romance, prestige, tradition, and a lot of inherited advice that sounds sensible until you look at the materials and industry properly. 

In this video I break down seven of the most common jewellery myths people still believe, from engagement ring spending rules to lab-grown gemstones, precious metal hierarchies, and the idea that heavier automatically means better made.

I’m Mason, a jeweller and founder of Urban Sterling. On this channel I explore the materials scien

themes.name: The Origin Story
themes.tagline: How did we get here? The surprisingly wild science behind things that shape our world.

items.title: How HIV Started a Century Before Anyone Noticed
items.description:
↓↓↓ More info and sources below ↓↓↓

For decades, one man was blamed for bringing AIDS to America. But the "Patient Zero" story was wrong. This is the story of how genetic detective work traced HIV's true origins—from chimpanzees in Central Africa to a global pandemic—and how science unraveled one of the biggest myths in modern medicine.

Be Smart explores science questions you didn’t even know you wondered about. It is hosted and produced by Joe Hanson, PhD. We post new videos all the time, so

items.title: Is Da' Bomb Actually The Hottest Hot Sauce In The World?
items.description:
Think you can handle Da’ Bomb? Well, think again! The infamous #8 hot sauce from Hot Ones is so incredibly spicy it’s manufacturer warns people against eating it straight. So why does Hot Ones host Sean Evans have celebrities like Sabrina Carpenter taking it head-on?

In this episode of Weird History Food, we give you a glimpse of the shocking truth behind one of the most feared and misunderstood hot sauces on YouTube, discuss how it became a viral challenge, and try to figure out why famous peo

morning_soundtrack.title: Radio Free Aquarium Drunkard :: July 2026
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Freeform transmissions from Radio Free Aquarium Drunkard on dublab. Airing every third Sunday of the month, RFAD on dublab features the pairing of Tyler Wilcox’s Doom and Gloom from the Tomb and Chad DePasquale’s New Happy Gathering. This month, Tyler kicks it off with a mix of cosmic summer jazz, n
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Like drifting through a late-night FM signal that keeps shifting between sun-bleached jazz and something stranger, best heard with the windows down at 2am.

morning_soundtrack.title: Pavement Perform Clunky Cover of “Stairway to Heaven”: Watch
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
The intentionally disastrous rendition was staged at Mosswood Meltdown.
Pavement Perform Clunky Cover of “Stairway to Heaven”: Watch
Travis Bland
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The gleeful chaos of watching someone deliberately knock over a tower of blocks — messy, loud, and absolutely worth it.

morning_soundtrack.title: Olaf Rupp’s Latest Adventure in Avant-Garde Solo Guitar
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Olaf Rupp's Fuzzy Logic is yet another fascinating release in the Palilalia Records catalog, and his work here is impressive and deeply satisfying.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The sound of electricity thinking out loud in an empty room, rewarding for anyone willing to sit still and really listen.

global_silver_linings.title: Pigeon Walks Up to Firefighters Who Offer Oxygen to ‘Clear its Lungs’
global_silver_linings.snippet: Video footage of a fireman giving oxygen to a pigeon following a fire has gone viral on social media. After attending to a vehicle fire on Wednesday evening, the E29 crew of Oakland Firefighters noticed a pigeon in distress. “The crew reported, the pigeon came up to them seeking help.
global_silver_linings.reason: A pigeon instinctively seeking help from firefighters — and the crew pausing to treat it — is a small but genuine reminder that compassion for all living things is alive and well. These moments of unlikely connection between humans and wildlife are worth cherishing.

global_silver_linings.title: 27 English words people have a hard time enunciating properly, even native speakers
global_silver_linings.snippet: Say what? The post 27 English words people have a hard time enunciating properly, even native speakers appeared first on Upworthy .
global_silver_linings.reason: Language is endlessly humbling, and there's something quietly unifying about the fact that even lifelong English speakers stumble over the same words. It's a gentle nudge that nobody has it all figured out, and that's perfectly fine.

global_silver_linings.title: What watching soccer does to your body, according to science
global_silver_linings.snippet: BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Forty-eight teams. Sixty-four matches. A final played yesterday at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey.
global_silver_linings.reason: Science is finding that shared experiences like watching sport can have measurable positive effects on our bodies and sense of belonging — a good reminder that community and joy are not just nice to have, but genuinely good for us.

discovery_articles.title: The Darß Front Doors Path in Prerow, Germany
discovery_articles.snippet: Very special front doors can be found on the Fischland-Darß-Zingst peninsula in Mecklenburg-Western Pomerania. These richly decorated and brightly painted doors date back to the 18th century. Since 2018, they are officially part of UNESCO World Heritage.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: These aren't merely decorative — the vivid, intricately carved doors of the Darß peninsula were a fisherman's way of broadcasting identity and pride in a world where most things were salt-bleached and plain. UNESCO agreeing, three centuries later, feels like a long-overdue knock on the right door.

discovery_articles.title: Tongyili & Tongdeli in Su Zhou Shi, China
discovery_articles.snippet: The Green Gang, possibly the biggest and most influential mafia in modern China, are mostly associated with the glitz and glamour of metropolitan Shanghai in the 1920s and 30s. However, some key figures at the very top of the gang’s hierarchy have their roots in the sleepy, nearby water town of Suzhou. Just 70 miles west of Shanghai, Suzhou doesn’t have quite the rich early-20th-century gangster history that Shanghai can boast.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: The Green Gang ran Shanghai's underworld with terrifying efficiency, yet its most powerful bosses quietly kept their roots in a genteel canal town known for silk and scholars — the kind of origin story that makes you look twice at any sleepy backwater. Suzhou's still waters, it turns out, ran very deep.

discovery_articles.title: Dongfangzhimen - The Big Pants Building in Suzhou Shi, China
discovery_articles.snippet: It is said that China builds, on average, a new skyscraper every five days. And, with the country's continued efforts to forge forward with the growth of mega-cities seemingly overnight, some of those buildings aren’t going to please everyone. Nearly half of the twenty tallest buildings in the world currently reside in mainland China, and burgeoning new cities want to keep up with the ultra-modernist trends they have seen in Shanghai and Beijing.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: A skyscraper every five days is staggering enough, but the real detail here is that China's breakneck skyline ambitions have produced buildings so architecturally audacious — or baffling — that locals nicknamed one 'the Big Pants.' Modernism at that velocity apparently has its own dress code.

discovery_articles.title: The Dog Line in Eaglehawk Neck, Australia
discovery_articles.snippet: Savage dogs chained up in a line guarded against prisoners escaping from Port Arthur in the 1800s. The dog line, which ran across the 100-meter-wide "Neck" at Eaglehawk, was devised by John Peyton Jones. Any movement or the slightest noise would set the dogs barking and alert the sentries to ensure that no convicts escaped across the narrow stretch of land that led from the Port Arthur convict settlement further south.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: A hundred meters of chained, snarling dogs was all that stood between hundreds of desperate convicts and freedom — a psychological barrier as much as a physical one, since the mere sound of barking was the real lock. It's a grimly ingenious solution that says everything about how Port Arthur's keepers understood fear.

featured_read.title: How to Be a Lichen: Adaptive Strategies for the Vulnerabilities of Being Human from Nature’s Tiny Titans of Tenacity
featured_read.snippet: When I was a child, little delighted me more than the magical green garlands draping from the pine trees, which I made into wreaths and mustaches to roam the mountains of Bulgaria as a miniature Orlando. I had no idea that Usnea longissima is just one of more than 20,000 known species of lichen — almost twice as many as birds. In the lifetime since, I have collected and photographed lichen all over the world, from the spruces lining the wild shores of Alaska to the stone walls lining the rural roads of Ireland, from Basquiat’s grave in Brooklyn’s Green-Wood… read article
featured_read.blurb: This essay uses the quiet, stubborn life of lichen — part fungus, part algae, wholly neither — to ask what it might mean to thrive not despite vulnerability but through an entirely different arrangement of self. Maria Popova turns a childhood mustache of Bulgarian moss into a meditation on symbiosis, resilience, and the strange dignity of being an in-between thing.

garden_note.note: First light crept over the Zürichberg at 05:12 this morning, and the garden is already humming — roses in their second flush are heavy with dew, and the sweet corn is doing that midsummer thing where you can almost hear it growing. High summer in Zürich asks very little of you today except to step outside early and simply look.
in_season: Repeat-flowering roses (deadhead after the first flush to encourage the next)
Courgettes — harvest small for tenderness, check daily
Lavender at peak bloom, ready to cut and dry
Runner beans climbing fast, keep picking to keep them producing
garden_note.sky_tonight: After dusk around 21:52, look southwest for the waxing crescent Moon low on the horizon — Venus may be keeping it company as the sky deepens.
garden_note.sun_times: The sun rises at 05:48 this morning and lingers until 21:16 tonight — a generous 15½ hours of light.

puzzle.label: The Morning Haiku
puzzle.prompt:
open range
____ grow through
a cowboy’s hat

(Fill in the missing word.)
puzzle.hint: a prickly wildflower that doesn't ask permission
puzzle.credit: Haiku by Debbie Strange, via tinywords.com

previous_puzzle.label: The Evening Enigma
previous_puzzle.prompt: A man carefully reads every word on the menu, orders confidently, and yet when the food arrives he cannot eat a single thing on his plate. The restaurant has made no mistakes, and the man is not ill. Why not?
previous_puzzle.answer: The man is a professional food critic who ordered on behalf of his dining companion — the meal was always intended for someone else.

news.title: Melon Tomato Salad
news.snippet: Two words: Tajín vinaigrette. READ MORE...
news.blurb: A Tajín vinaigrette on melon and tomato is the kind of summer pairing that makes you wonder why you ever ate either fruit any other way.

news.title: Rachel Roddy’s homage to Michèle Roberts’ recipe for chicken saute with tomatoes and mushrooms
news.snippet: This Napoleonic classic is all too often overcomplicated, but this ode to the French-British author’s version is both simple and stunning A few weeks ago, as part of the British Library’s food season , the novelist Michèle Roberts , biographer Francesca Wade , writer Eli Davies and food writer Rebecca May Johnson were brought together for a discussion on women’s culinary lives, and on the kitchen as a space of creativity, resistance and intellectual life. I couldn’t be there, but by all accounts it was a brilliant discussion, which I hope was recorded. I have, though, read all four authors’ recent books.
news.blurb: A Napoleonic classic stripped of its fuss — Rachel Roddy's quiet homage proves that the best cooking is often just getting out of the recipe's way.

news.title: What’s the Point of a Food Pyramid Anyway?
news.snippet: Noemi Fabra The U.S. dietary guidelines—including their recent updates—fail to acknowledge the diversity of human needs and values. The post What’s the Point of a Food Pyramid Anyway?
news.blurb: A sharp, timely look at why a government-issued triangle was never quite equipped to hold the full, glorious complexity of how humans actually eat.

recipe.title: Cherry Tomato Tart with Burnt Scallion Ricotta
recipe.snippet: This cherry tomato tart starts with an all-butter crust spread with a thin swipe of chili crisp, then filled with ricotta folded with deeply roasted scallions and fresh chives. A generous layer blistered cherry tomatoes tops the ricotta, and a final scattering of toasted almonds brings added crunch. Bake this tart, share with friends!
recipe.blurb: With an all-butter crust, a swipe of chili crisp, ricotta folded with deeply roasted scallions and fresh chives, and blistered cherry tomatoes on top, this tart is peak summer with very little standing between you and something genuinely beautiful.

larder.seasonal_note: Zürich's Tuesday and Saturday markets at Bürkliplatz are spilling over with fat Thurgau apricots right now — worth arriving early to snag a kilo for a lazy Marillenknödel project, or simply eaten warm from the bag on the walk home.

fern_data.greeting: Wednesday has arrived with the mild audacity of a season that knows it has nowhere to be. Today we're untangling whether Da' Bomb hot sauce is actually hellfire or just hype, tracing how HIV quietly took a century's head start on everyone, and learning from a lichen how to be extraordinarily resilient while looking, frankly, like nothing much.
